docs.title: Dashforge
docs.text: A JSON-first dashboard framework that starts simple with static hosting and grows into a full Metabase-like analytics platform with an AI-powered visual builder.

docs.title: Why Dashforge?
docs.text:
• Visual Dashboard Builder - Drag-and-drop editor optimized for both humans and LLM agents
  • JSON IR First - Dashboards are defined in a non-polymorphic, AI-friendly JSON format that's easy to generate, validate, and version control
  • Cube.js Semantic Layer - Business-friendly queries with pre-built relationships and AI context
  • Static to Dynamic - Start with static file hosting (GitHub Pages), graduate to a full PostgreSQL-backed server when you need it
  • Multi-tenant Ready - Built-in Row Level Security for SaaS deployments
  • SSO Authentication - GitHub and Google OAuth out of the box
  • ChartIR Integration - Uses echartify for declarative chart definitions

docs.title: Builder Mode
docs.text:
Visual drag-and-drop dashboard creation:

  • 12-column responsive grid layout
  • Widget palette with charts, metrics, tables, and text
  • Real-time chart preview with ECharts
  • Cube.js query builder for semantic queries
  • AI-powered widget generation from natural language
cd builder && npm install && npm run build && cd ..
./dashforge-server serve --port 8080
open http://localhost:8080/builder/

docs.title: Static Mode
docs.text:
Perfect for:

  • Documentation sites
  • GitHub Pages hosting
  • Simple data visualization
  • No backend required
# Just open in a browser
open viewer/index.html?dashboard=./examples/dashboard.json

docs.title: Server Mode
docs.text:
Full-featured analytics platform with:

  • PostgreSQL data sources
  • User authentication (OAuth)
  • Multi-tenant isolation
  • Dashboard CRUD API
  • Saved queries
./dashforge-server serve --database-url postgres://... --auto-migrate

docs.title: AI-First Design
docs.text:
Dashforge is designed from the ground up to work seamlessly with LLM agents:

  • Non-polymorphic JSON - Dashboard IR uses flat structures without oneOf/anyOf, making it easy for AI to generate valid configurations
  • Semantic context - Cube.js provides business-friendly names like "revenue" and "customer_count" instead of raw column names
  • Validated generation - JSON Schema validation ensures AI-generated output is always valid
  • Dual rendering - Same ChartIR spec works in the dashboard builder and chatbot responses
User: "Add a line chart showing revenue trends"
  ↓
AI generates ChartIR JSON
  ↓
Validation passes
  ↓
Widget added to dashboard

docs.title: AI Features
docs.text: Dashforge is designed from the ground up to work with LLM agents. The non-polymorphic JSON format, semantic data layer, and validation system make it easy for AI to generate valid dashboard configurations.

docs.title: Non-polymorphic JSON
docs.text:
Traditional dashboard formats use polymorphic schemas:

// Polymorphic (hard for AI)
{
  "type": "chart",
  "config": { ... }  // Structure depends on chart type
}

Dashforge uses flat, explicit structures:

// Non-polymorphic (easy for AI)
{
  "type": "chart",
  "config": {
    "geometry": "line",
    "encodings": { "x": "date", "y": "revenue" },
    "style": { "smooth": true }
  }
}

docs.title: Semantic Context
docs.text:
Cube.js provides business context that helps AI understand the data:

Raw SQL (confusing):
SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', o.created_at), SUM(o.amount)
FROM orders o GROUP BY 1

Semantic (clear):
Orders.totalRevenue by Orders.createdAt (monthly)

docs.title: Validated Generation
docs.text:
All AI-generated configurations are validated before use:

User prompt → AI generates JSON → Validation → Dashboard/Widget
                                      ↓
                              Error feedback → AI retry

docs.title: 1. Generate Dashboard from Description
docs.text:
Create a complete dashboard from a natural language description:

User: "Create a sales dashboard with revenue by region and monthly trends"

Generated:
- Title: "Sales Dashboard"
- Widgets:
  - Revenue by Region (bar chart)
  - Monthly Revenue Trend (line chart)
  - Total Revenue (metric)
  - Top Products (table)

docs.title: 2. Add Widget from Description
docs.text:
Add a single widget to an existing dashboard:

User: "Add a pie chart showing sales by category"

Generated widget:
{
  "type": "chart",
  "title": "Sales by Category",
  "position": { "x": 0, "y": 6, "w": 4, "h": 3 },
  "config": {
    "geometry": "pie",
    "encodings": { "category": "category", "value": "sales" }
  }
}

docs.title: 3. Modify Existing Widget
docs.text:
Update a widget based on instructions:

User: "Change the bar chart to show percentages"

Before:
{ "geometry": "bar", "encodings": { "y": "revenue" } }

After:
{ "geometry": "bar", "encodings": { "y": "revenue_pct" }, "style": { "format": "percent" } }

docs.title: 4. Natural Language Queries
docs.text:
Generate Cube.js queries from questions:

User: "Which products had the highest profit margin last quarter?"

Generated query:
{
  "measures": ["Products.profitMargin"],
  "dimensions": ["Products.name"],
  "timeDimensions": [{
    "dimension": "Orders.createdAt",
    "dateRange": "last quarter"
  }],
  "order": { "Products.profitMargin": "desc" },
  "limit": 10
}

docs.title: JSON Schemas
docs.text: The AI uses JSON Schema definitions to ensure valid output.

docs.title: Dashboard Schema
docs.text:
{
  "type": "object",
  "required": ["title", "widgets"],
  "properties": {
    "title": { "type": "string" },
    "layout": {
      "type": "object",
      "properties": {
        "type": { "enum": ["grid"] },
        "columns": { "type": "integer", "default": 12 }
      }
    },
    "widgets": {
      "type": "array",
      "items": { "$ref": "#/$defs/Widget" }
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Widget Schema
docs.text:
{
  "type": "object",
  "required": ["type", "position"],
  "properties": {
    "type": { "enum": ["chart", "metric", "table", "text"] },
    "title": { "type": "string" },
    "position": {
      "type": "object",
      "properties": {
        "x": { "type": "integer", "minimum": 0 },
        "y": { "type": "integer", "minimum": 0 },
        "w": { "type": "integer", "minimum": 1, "maximum": 12 },
        "h": { "type": "integer", "minimum": 1 }
      }
    }
  }
}

docs.title: ChartConfig Schema
docs.text:
{
  "type": "object",
  "required": ["geometry"],
  "properties": {
    "geometry": { "enum": ["line", "bar", "pie", "scatter", "area"] },
    "encodings": {
      "type": "object",
      "properties": {
        "x": { "type": "string" },
        "y": { "type": "string" },
        "color": { "type": "string" },
        "value": { "type": "string" },
        "category": { "type": "string" }
      }
    },
    "style": {
      "type": "object",
      "properties": {
        "showLegend": { "type": "boolean" },
        "smooth": { "type": "boolean" },
        "stack": { "type": "boolean" }
      }
    }
  }
}

docs.title: System Prompts
docs.text: The builder includes optimized prompts for dashboard generation.

docs.title: Dashboard Generation Prompt
docs.text:
You are a dashboard design assistant. You help users create data dashboards
by generating JSON configurations.

You output JSON that follows the DashboardIR specification. Key rules:
- Use a 12-column grid layout
- Position widgets using x, y, w, h coordinates
- Common widget sizes: metrics (2x2), charts (4x3 or 6x3), tables (6x4)
- Align widgets to avoid overlap
- Use descriptive titles for widgets

Chart types: line, bar, pie, scatter, area
Widget types: chart, metric, table, text

Always respond with valid JSON only.

docs.title: Widget Generation Prompt
docs.text:
The prompt includes:

  1. Schema definition
  2. Existing widget positions (to avoid overlap)
  3. Available data fields (from Cube.js schema)
  4. User's request

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: All AI-generated output is validated before use.

docs.title: Validation Steps
docs.text:
1. JSON Parse: Ensure valid JSON syntax
  2. Schema Validation: Check against JSON Schema
  3. Semantic Validation: Verify field references exist
  4. Layout Validation: Check for widget overlaps

docs.title: Auto-fix
docs.text:
Common issues are automatically fixed:

  • Missing IDs are generated
  • Negative positions are set to 0
  • Widths exceeding 12 are capped
  • Missing required fields use defaults

docs.title: Error Feedback
docs.text:
If validation fails, errors are returned to the AI for retry:

{
  "valid": false,
  "errors": [
    "Position x cannot be negative",
    "Chart geometry must be one of: line, bar, pie, scatter, area"
  ],
  "warnings": [
    "Widget has no title"
  ]
}

docs.title: AI Generation Endpoint
docs.text:
POST /api/v1/ai/generate
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "prompt": "Create a sales dashboard",
  "type": "dashboard",
  "schema": { ... },  // Optional: Cube.js schema for context
  "options": {
    "model": "claude-3-sonnet",
    "temperature": 0.7
  }
}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "success": true,
  "data": {
    "title": "Sales Dashboard",
    "widgets": [ ... ]
  },
  "warnings": []
}

docs.title: Mock Generation
docs.text:
For development without an AI backend, the builder includes mock generation:

import { mockGenerateWidget } from './api/ai'

const result = await mockGenerateWidget(
  "Add a line chart showing trends",
  existingWidgets
)
// Returns a basic widget based on keyword matching

docs.title: For Prompt Engineering
docs.text:
1. Be specific about data fields: "revenue by region" not "sales data"
  2. Specify chart types when possible: "bar chart" not "visualization"
  3. Include time ranges: "last 6 months" not "recent"
  4. Mention comparisons: "vs previous period"

docs.title: For AI Integration
docs.text:
1. Always validate generated output
  2. Provide schema context from Cube.js
  3. Include existing widget positions to avoid overlap
  4. Use temperature 0.7 for creativity, 0.3 for precision
  5. Implement retry logic for validation failures

docs.title: API Reference
docs.text: The Dashforge REST API provides programmatic access to dashboards, data sources, and queries.

docs.title: Base URL
docs.text: https://your-domain.com/api/v1

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
Most endpoints require authentication via JWT Bearer token:

curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <access_token>" \
  https://dashforge.example.com/api/v1/dashboards

docs.title: Response Format
docs.text:
All responses use JSON:

{
  "data": { ... },
  "meta": {
    "total": 100,
    "page": 1,
    "perPage": 20
  }
}

Error responses:

{
  "error": {
    "code": "NOT_FOUND",
    "message": "Dashboard not found"
  }
}

docs.title: Check Health
docs.text:
GET /health

No authentication required.

Response:

{
  "status": "ok"
}

docs.text:
POST /api/v1/datasources

Request:

{
  "name": "Analytics DB",
  "slug": "analytics-db",
  "type": "postgres",
  "connectionUrl": "postgres://user:pass@db.example.com:5432/analytics?sslmode=require",
  "maxConnections": 10,
  "queryTimeoutSeconds": 30,
  "readOnly": true,
  "sslEnabled": true,
  "active": true
}
Field Type Required Description name string Yes Display name slug string Yes URL-safe identifier (unique) type string Yes Provider type (postgres, mysql) connectionUrl string * Connection string connectionUrlEnv string * Env var containing connection URL maxConnections int No Max pool size (default: 10) queryTimeoutSeconds int No Query timeout (default: 30) readOnly bool No Restrict to SELECT queries sslEnabled bool No Enable SSL/TLS active bool No Enable data source

*Either connectionUrl or connectionUrlEnv is required.

Response: 201 Created

{
  "id": 2,
  "name": "Analytics DB",
  "slug": "analytics-db",
  "type": "postgres",
  "active": true,

docs.title: Execute Query
docs.text:
POST /api/v1/datasources/:id/query

Request:

{
  "query": "SELECT date, SUM(amount) as total FROM sales WHERE region = :region GROUP BY date",
  "parameters": {
    "region": "US"
  },
  "maxRows": 1000
}
Field Type Required Description query string Yes SQL query parameters object No Named parameters (:name or @name) maxRows int No Max rows to return (default: 1000, max: 10000)

Response:

{
  "columns": [
    {"name": "date", "type": "DATE", "nullable": false},
    {"name": "total", "type": "NUMERIC", "nullable": true, "precision": 10, "scale": 2}
  ],
  "rows": [
    {"date": "2024-01-01", "total": 15000.50},
    {"date": "2024-01-02", "total": 18500.75}
  ],
  "rowCount": 2,
  "executionTimeMs": 125
}

Read-Only Data Sources

If the data source has readOnly: true, only SELECT queries are allowed. INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE will return a 400 error.

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text: Dashforge supports OAuth 2.0 authentication with GitHub and Google providers, using JWT tokens for session management.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
┌─────────┐     ┌───────────┐     ┌──────────────┐
│ Browser │────▶│ Dashforge │────▶│ GitHub/Google│
│         │◀────│  Server   │◀────│    OAuth     │
└─────────┘     └───────────┘     └──────────────┘
     │                │
     │  JWT Tokens    │
     └────────────────┘
  1. User clicks "Login with GitHub/Google"
  2. Dashforge redirects to OAuth provider
  3. User authenticates with provider
  4. Provider redirects back with authorization code
  5. Dashforge exchanges code for user info
  6. Dashforge creates/updates user and returns JWT tokens

docs.title: GitHub
docs.text:
1. Go to GitHub Developer Settings
  2. Click "New OAuth App"
  3. Fill in:
  4. Application name: Dashforge
  5. Homepage URL: https://your-domain.com
  6. Authorization callback URL: https://your-domain.com/api/v1/auth/github/callback
  7. Save and note the Client ID and Client Secret
export GITHUB_CLIENT_ID="your-client-id"
export GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET="your-client-secret"

docs.title: Google
docs.text:
1. Go to Google Cloud Console
  2. Create a new project or select existing
  3. Go to "Credentials" → "Create Credentials" → "OAuth client ID"
  4. Configure consent screen if prompted
  5. Select "Web application"
  6. Add authorized redirect URI: https://your-domain.com/api/v1/auth/google/callback
  7. Note the Client ID and Client Secret
export GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID="your-client-id"
export GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET="your-client-secret"

docs.title: Initiate Login
docs.text:
Redirect users to start the OAuth flow:

GET /api/v1/auth/github
GET /api/v1/auth/google

Optional query parameter:

  • redirect: URL to redirect after successful login

Example:

<a href="/api/v1/auth/github?redirect=/dashboard">Login with GitHub</a>

docs.title: OAuth Callbacks
docs.text:
These are called by the OAuth provider (not directly by users):

GET /api/v1/auth/github/callback
GET /api/v1/auth/google/callback

docs.title: Get Current User
docs.text:
GET /api/v1/auth/me
Authorization: Bearer <access_token>

Response:

{
  "id": 1,
  "email": "user@example.com",
  "name": "John Doe",
  "role": "viewer",
  "active": true,
  "lastLoginAt": "2024-01-15T10:30:00Z",
  "createdAt": "2024-01-01T00:00:00Z"
}

docs.title: Refresh Tokens
docs.text:
POST /api/v1/auth/refresh
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "refreshToken": "your-refresh-token"
}

Response:

{
  "accessToken": "new-access-token",
  "refreshToken": "new-refresh-token",
  "expiresIn": 900,
  "tokenType": "Bearer"
}

docs.title: Logout
docs.text:
POST /api/v1/auth/logout
Authorization: Bearer <access_token>

Returns 204 No Content.

docs.title: Token Structure
docs.text:
Access tokens contain:

{
  "iss": "dashforge",
  "sub": "1",
  "exp": 1705312200,
  "iat": 1705311300,
  "uid": 1,
  "email": "user@example.com",
  "role": "admin",
  "tid": 1
}
Claim Description iss Issuer (always "dashforge") sub Subject (user ID as string) exp Expiration timestamp iat Issued at timestamp uid User ID (number) email User email role User role tid Tenant ID (for multi-tenancy)

docs.title: Using Tokens
docs.text:
Include the access token in the Authorization header:

curl -H "Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGc..." \
  https://dashforge.example.com/api/v1/dashboards

docs.title: Token Expiration
docs.text: Token Type Default Lifetime Access Token 15 minutes Refresh Token 7 days

docs.title: User Roles
docs.text:
Dashforge uses role-based access control:

Role Permissions viewer View dashboards, run saved queries editor viewer + create/edit dashboards admin editor + manage users, data sources owner admin + tenant settings, billing

docs.title: Role Hierarchy
docs.text: owner > admin > editor > viewer

docs.title: Protecting API Routes
docs.text:
Authenticated routes require a valid JWT:

// Protected route
mux.Handle("/api/v1/dashboards",
    jwtService.Middleware(dashboardHandler))

// Role-restricted route
mux.Handle("/api/v1/admin/users",
    jwtService.Middleware(
        auth.RequireJWTRole("admin", "owner")(userHandler)))

docs.title: JWT Secret
docs.text:
• Use a cryptographically random secret (minimum 32 bytes)
  • Never commit secrets to version control
  • Rotate secrets periodically
  • Use environment variables or secrets manager
# Generate secure secret
openssl rand -base64 32

docs.title: HTTPS
docs.text:
Always use HTTPS in production:

export BASE_URL="https://dashforge.example.com"

docs.title: Cookie Security
docs.text:
OAuth state cookies are configured with:

  • HttpOnly: Prevents JavaScript access
  • Secure: Only sent over HTTPS
  • SameSite=Lax: CSRF protection
  • MaxAge=600: 10-minute expiration

docs.title: CORS
docs.text:
Configure CORS for your frontend domain:

# config.yaml
cors:
  allowed_origins:
    - https://app.example.com
  allowed_methods:
    - GET
    - POST
    - PUT
    - DELETE
  allowed_headers:
    - Authorization
    - Content-Type

docs.title: Disabling Authentication
docs.text:
For development only:

./dashforge-server serve --disable-auth

Warning

Never disable authentication in production. This flag is for local development only.

docs.title: Authorization
docs.text: Dashforge provides flexible authorization with two modes: simple role hierarchy and SpiceDB for fine-grained control.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Dashforge has two distinct organization types with separate role hierarchies:

  • Publishers - Organizations that create and sell dashboard templates
  • Consumers - Organizations that use Dashforge for their own dashboards

docs.title: Publisher Roles
docs.text:
For organizations creating and selling templates:

Role Level Permissions Owner 100 Full control, billing, delete organization Admin 80 Manage members, publish templates, view analytics Creator 60 Create templates and data connectors Reviewer 40 Review templates before publishing

docs.title: Consumer Roles
docs.text:
For organizations using Dashforge:

Role Level Permissions Owner 100 Full control, billing, delete organization Admin 80 Manage members, data sources, integrations Editor 60 Create/edit dashboards, queries, alerts Viewer 40 View dashboards and data

docs.title: Simple Mode (Default)
docs.text:
Role-based hierarchy with numeric levels:

// Check if user can perform action
if authz.CheckMinRole(ctx, user, "editor") {
    // User has editor role or higher
}

// Check specific permission
if authz.Can(ctx, user, "dashboard:edit", dashboardID) {
    // User can edit this dashboard
}

docs.title: SpiceDB Mode
docs.text:
Fine-grained relationship-based authorization:

# Enable SpiceDB in config
authorization:
  provider: spicedb
  spicedb:
    endpoint: "localhost:50051"
    preshared_key: "your-key"

docs.title: Dashboard Permissions
docs.text: Permission Description Roles view View dashboard Viewer+ edit Edit dashboard Editor+ delete Delete dashboard Admin+ share Share with others Editor+ export Export dashboard Viewer+

docs.title: Data Source Permissions
docs.text: Permission Description Roles view View connection Viewer+ use Query data Editor+ manage Create/edit/delete Admin+

docs.title: Template Permissions (Publishers)
docs.text: Permission Description Roles create Create templates Creator+ edit Edit templates Creator+ publish Publish to marketplace Admin+ delete Delete templates Admin+

docs.title: SpiceDB Schema
docs.text:
The full authorization schema defines relationships between resources:

definition platform {
    relation admin: principal
    relation marketplace_moderator: principal
    permission manage = admin
    permission moderate_marketplace = marketplace_moderator + admin
}

definition publisher {
    relation platform: platform
    relation owner: principal
    relation admin: principal
    relation creator: principal
    relation reviewer: principal

    permission manage = owner
    permission publish = admin + owner
    permission create_template = creator + admin + owner
    permission review = reviewer + creator + admin + owner
}

definition organization {
    relation platform: platform
    relation owner: principal
    relation admin: principal
    relation editor: principal
    relation viewer: principal

    permission manage = owner
    permission admin_access = admin + owner
    permission edit = editor + admin + owner
    permission view = viewer + editor + admin + owner
}

definition dashboard {
    relation organization: organization
    relation owner: principal
    relation editor: principal
    relation viewer: principal

    permission manage = owner + organization->manage
    permission edit = editor + owner + organization->edit
    permission view = viewer + editor + owner + organization->view
    permission share = editor + owner + organization->admin_access
    permission export = view
}

docs.title: In HTTP Handlers
docs.text:
func (h *Handler) UpdateDashboard(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
    ctx := r.Context()
    dashboardID := chi.URLParam(r, "id")

    // Check permission
    if !h.authz.Can(ctx, "dashboard", dashboardID, "edit") {
        http.Error(w, "Forbidden", http.StatusForbidden)
        return
    }

    // ... update logic
}

docs.title: Filtering Resources
docs.text:
// Get only dashboards user can view
dashboards, err := h.authz.Filter(ctx, "dashboard", "view", allDashboardIDs)

docs.title: Bulk Permission Check
docs.text:
// Check multiple permissions at once
results, err := h.authz.CanAll(ctx, []authz.Check{
    {Resource: "dashboard", ID: id1, Permission: "edit"},
    {Resource: "dashboard", ID: id2, Permission: "edit"},
})

docs.title: Relationship Sync
docs.text:
When identity changes occur, relationships are synced to SpiceDB:

Event Action User joins org Add organization#viewer@user User role changes Update relation User leaves org Remove all org relations Dashboard created Add dashboard#owner@user Dashboard shared Add viewer/editor relation

docs.title: Platform Admin
docs.text:
Platform admins have cross-organization access:

if authz.IsPlatformAdmin(ctx, user) {
    // Full access to all resources
}

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Principle of Least Privilege - Start with Viewer, elevate as needed
  2. Use Groups - Manage permissions through organization roles
  3. Audit Access - Log permission checks for compliance
  4. Test Thoroughly - Verify permission boundaries in tests
  5. Migrate Carefully - When switching to SpiceDB, run in audit mode first

docs.title: Dashboard Builder
docs.text: The Dashforge Dashboard Builder is a visual drag-and-drop editor for creating dashboards. It's built with React and TypeScript, and is optimized for both human users and LLM agents.

docs.title: Building the Builder
docs.text:
cd builder
npm install
npm run build
cd ..

docs.title: Running in Development Mode
docs.text:
For hot-reloading during development:

cd builder
npm run dev
# Opens http://localhost:5173

The dev server proxies API requests to http://localhost:8080, so start the Dashforge server in another terminal:

go run ./cmd/dashforge-server serve --port 8080

docs.title: Running in Production
docs.text:
Build and embed in the Dashforge server:

cd builder && npm run build && cd ..
go run ./cmd/dashforge-server serve --port 8080
# Builder available at http://localhost:8080/builder/

docs.title: Widget Palette
docs.text: Drag widgets from the left sidebar onto the canvas.

docs.title: Chart Widgets
docs.text: Widget Description Default Size Bar Chart Categorical comparisons 4x3 Line Chart Time series and trends 6x3 Area Chart Stacked time series 6x3 Pie Chart Part-to-whole relationships 4x3 Scatter Plot Correlations between variables 4x3

docs.title: Data Widgets
docs.text: Widget Description Default Size Metric Single KPI with optional sparkline 2x2 Table Tabular data with sorting/pagination 6x4

docs.title: Content Widgets
docs.text: Widget Description Default Size Text Markdown or plain text content 4x2 Image Static images 3x3

docs.title: Canvas
docs.text:
The canvas uses a 12-column grid layout, similar to Bootstrap:

  • Grid columns: 12 (configurable)
  • Row height: 80px (configurable)
  • Gap: 8px between widgets
  • Snap-to-grid: Widgets snap to grid positions

docs.title: Widget Operations
docs.text:
• Select: Click a widget to edit its properties
  • Move: Drag the grip handle in the widget header
  • Resize: Drag the bottom-right corner
  • Delete: Click the trash icon in the header
  • Duplicate: Click the copy icon in the header

docs.title: Keyboard Shortcuts
docs.text: Key Action Ctrl+Z Undo Ctrl+Shift+Z Redo Delete Delete selected widget Ctrl+D Duplicate selected widget

docs.title: Properties Panel
docs.text: When a widget is selected, the properties panel shows configuration options.

docs.title: General Properties
docs.text:
All widgets have:

  • Title: Display title shown in the widget header
  • Description: Optional description (tooltip)
  • Data Source: Which data source to use

docs.title: Chart Properties
docs.text:
Chart widgets have additional options:

  • Chart Type: line, bar, pie, scatter, area
  • Data Mapping:
    • X Axis / Category field
    • Y Axis / Value field
    • Color field (optional)
  • Style Options:
    • Color palette selection
    • Show/hide legend
    • Legend position (top, bottom, left, right)
    • Smooth lines (line/area charts)
    • Stacked (bar/area charts)
    • Horizontal (bar charts)

docs.title: Metric Properties
docs.text:
• Value Field: The field containing the metric value
  • Format: number, currency, percent, compact
  • Prefix/Suffix: Text to display before/after the value
  • Comparison: Show change vs previous period
  • Sparkline: Show mini trend chart

docs.title: Table Properties
docs.text:
• Columns: Configure visible columns
  • Pagination: Enable with page size
  • Sortable: Allow column sorting
  • Filterable: Allow column filtering

docs.title: Data Sources
docs.text: The builder supports multiple data source types:

docs.title: Inline Data
docs.text:
Embed data directly in the dashboard JSON:

{
  "id": "sample-data",
  "type": "inline",
  "data": [
    { "month": "Jan", "revenue": 12500 },
    { "month": "Feb", "revenue": 15700 }
  ]
}

docs.title: URL Data
docs.text:
Fetch data from an HTTP endpoint:

{
  "id": "api-data",
  "type": "url",
  "url": "https://api.example.com/sales",
  "method": "GET"
}

docs.title: Cube.js Data
docs.text:
Query the semantic layer (requires Cube.js):

{
  "id": "cube-data",
  "type": "cube",
  "query": {
    "measures": ["Orders.totalRevenue"],
    "dimensions": ["Orders.createdAt"],
    "timeDimensions": [{
      "dimension": "Orders.createdAt",
      "granularity": "month"
    }]
  }
}

See Cube.js Integration for details.

docs.title: Export to JSON
docs.text: Click the settings menu and select "Export JSON" to download the dashboard as a JSON file.

docs.title: Save to Server
docs.text: If connected to a Dashforge server, click "Save" to persist the dashboard to the database.

docs.title: Import from JSON
docs.text: Click "Import JSON" to load a dashboard from a file.

docs.title: Undo/Redo
docs.text:
The builder maintains a history of changes:

  • Up to 50 states are stored
  • Use Ctrl+Z / Ctrl+Shift+Z or the toolbar buttons
  • History is reset when loading a new dashboard

docs.title: Technology Stack
docs.text:
The builder is built with:

  • React 18 - UI framework
  • TypeScript - Type safety
  • Vite - Build tool
  • Tailwind CSS - Styling
  • Zustand - State management with undo/redo
  • React Query - Data fetching
  • react-grid-layout - Drag-and-drop grid
  • ECharts - Chart rendering via echarts-for-react
  • Cube.js Client - Semantic queries
  • Lucide React - Icons

docs.title: Changelog
docs.text:
All notable changes to DashForge are documented here.

The format is based on Keep a Changelog, this project adheres to Semantic Versioning, and commits follow Conventional Commits.

docs.text:
Initial release with full-stack dashboard builder.

:octicons-tag-24: Release Notes{ .md-button }

docs.title: Highlights
docs.text:

  • Ent-based database schema with OAuth authentication
  • Data source integrations and alert system

docs.title: Added
docs.text:

docs.title: Cube.js Integration
docs.text: Dashforge integrates with Cube.js to provide a semantic data layer. This enables business-friendly queries and provides rich context for AI-powered dashboard generation.

docs.title: For Users
docs.text:
• Business terms: Query "revenue" and "customers" instead of SUM(o.amount) and COUNT(DISTINCT u.id)
  • Pre-built relationships: Joins are defined in the model, no need to remember table relationships
  • Consistent metrics: Everyone uses the same definition of "revenue"

docs.title: For AI Agents
docs.text:
• Semantic context: LLMs understand business concepts better than raw SQL
  • Guardrails: Access control prevents unauthorized data access
  • Validated queries: Cube validates query structure before execution

docs.title: 1. Install Cube.js
docs.text:
cd cube
npm install

docs.title: 2. Configure Database Connection
docs.text:
Create .env in the cube/ directory:

# PostgreSQL
CUBEJS_DB_TYPE=postgres
CUBEJS_DB_HOST=localhost
CUBEJS_DB_PORT=5432
CUBEJS_DB_NAME=your_database
CUBEJS_DB_USER=your_user
CUBEJS_DB_PASS=your_password

# Or MySQL
# CUBEJS_DB_TYPE=mysql
# CUBEJS_DB_HOST=localhost
# ...

# API settings
CUBEJS_API_SECRET=your-api-secret

docs.title: 3. Start Cube.js
docs.text:
cd cube
npm run dev
# Cube.js API available at http://localhost:4000

docs.title: Data Modeling
docs.text: Cube.js data models are defined in YAML files in cube/model/cubes/.

docs.title: Example Cube
docs.text:
# cube/model/cubes/orders.yml
cubes:
  - name: Orders
    title: Orders
    description: Customer orders with revenue metrics

    sql: >
      SELECT * FROM orders

    joins:
      - name: Customers
        relationship: many_to_one
        sql: "{CUBE}.customer_id = {Customers}.id"

    measures:
      - name: count
        title: Total Orders
        type: count

      - name: totalRevenue
        title: Total Revenue
        type: sum
        sql: total_amount
        format: currency

      - name: averageOrderValue
        title: Average Order Value
        type: avg
        sql: total_amount
        format: currency

    dimensions:
      - name: id
        title: Order ID
        type: string
        sql: id
        primaryKey: true

      - name: status
        title: Status
        type: string
        sql: status

      - name: createdAt
        title: Created At
        type: time
        sql: created_at

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Concept Description Cube A logical entity representing a table or view Measure Aggregated values (SUM, COUNT, AVG, etc.) Dimension Attributes to group or filter by Join Relationship to another cube Segment Pre-defined filter conditions

docs.title: Query Builder
docs.text: The dashboard builder includes a visual query builder for Cube.js.

docs.title: Using the Query Builder
docs.text:
1. Select a cube from the dropdown
  2. Choose measures (numeric values to display)
  3. Choose dimensions (categories to group by)
  4. Add filters (optional)
  5. Click "Run Query" to preview results

docs.title: Query Format
docs.text:
Cube.js queries use JSON:

{
  "measures": ["Orders.totalRevenue", "Orders.count"],
  "dimensions": ["Orders.status"],
  "timeDimensions": [{
    "dimension": "Orders.createdAt",
    "granularity": "month",
    "dateRange": "last 6 months"
  }],
  "filters": [{
    "member": "Orders.status",
    "operator": "equals",
    "values": ["completed"]
  }],
  "order": {
    "Orders.totalRevenue": "desc"
  },
  "limit": 100
}

docs.title: Schema Browser
docs.text:
The schema browser shows all available cubes, measures, and dimensions with their descriptions.

Orders
├── Measures
│   ├── count (Total Orders) - count
│   ├── totalRevenue (Total Revenue) - sum, currency
│   └── averageOrderValue (Average Order Value) - avg, currency
├── Dimensions
│   ├── id (Order ID) - string 🔑
│   ├── status (Status) - string
│   └── createdAt (Created At) - time
└── Joins
    └── Customers (many_to_one)

docs.title: Dashboard Data Source
docs.text:
To use Cube.js data in a widget, create a data source with type cube:

{
  "dataSources": [{
    "id": "monthly-revenue",
    "type": "cube",
    "query": {
      "measures": ["Orders.totalRevenue"],
      "timeDimensions": [{
        "dimension": "Orders.createdAt",
        "granularity": "month",
        "dateRange": "last 12 months"
      }]
    }
  }],
  "widgets": [{
    "id": "revenue-chart",
    "type": "chart",
    "datasourceId": "monthly-revenue",
    "config": {
      "geometry": "line",
      "encodings": {
        "x": "Orders.createdAt",
        "y": "Orders.totalRevenue"
      }
    }
  }]
}

docs.title: Pre-aggregations
docs.text:
Cube.js can pre-aggregate data for faster queries:

cubes:
  - name: Orders
    # ... measures and dimensions ...

    preAggregations:
      - name: monthlyRevenue
        measures:
          - totalRevenue
          - count
        timeDimension: createdAt
        granularity: month
        refreshKey:
          every: 1 hour

docs.title: Multi-tenancy
docs.text:
Cube.js supports multi-tenant data isolation:

// cube/cube.js
module.exports = {
  contextToAppId: ({ securityContext }) => {
    return securityContext?.tenant_id || 'default';
  },

  queryRewrite: (query, { securityContext }) => {
    if (securityContext?.tenant_id) {
      query.filters = query.filters || [];
      query.filters.push({
        member: 'TenantBase.tenantId',
        operator: 'equals',
        values: [securityContext.tenant_id]
      });
    }
    return query;
  }
};

docs.title: AI Integration
docs.text:
Cube.js provides semantic context for AI-powered queries:

User: "Show me revenue by region for Q4"

AI receives schema context:
- Orders.totalRevenue (Total Revenue, currency)
- Orders.region (Region, string)
- Orders.createdAt (time dimension)

AI generates:
{
  "measures": ["Orders.totalRevenue"],
  "dimensions": ["Orders.region"],
  "timeDimensions": [{
    "dimension": "Orders.createdAt",
    "dateRange": ["2024-10-01", "2024-12-31"]
  }]
}

See AI Features for more details.

docs.title: Connection Issues
docs.text:
# Check if Cube.js is running
curl http://localhost:4000/cubejs-api/v1/meta

# Check database connection
cd cube && npm run dev -- --debug

docs.title: Query Errors
docs.text:
• Ensure measure/dimension names match exactly (case-sensitive)
  • Check that joins are defined for cross-cube queries
  • Verify time dimension format for date ranges

docs.title: Performance
docs.text:
• Use pre-aggregations for frequently-accessed queries
  • Add indexes to database columns used in dimensions
  • Consider query caching in Cube.js config

docs.title: Dashboard IR Reference
docs.text: The Dashboard Intermediate Representation (IR) is the JSON format that defines dashboards in Dashforge.

docs.title: Design Principles
docs.text:
1. Non-polymorphic - Each field has a single type, making it easy for code generation
  2. AI-friendly - Structured for LLM generation and manipulation
  3. Progressive - Simple dashboards require minimal configuration
  4. Composable - Data sources, transforms, and widgets can be combined flexibly

docs.title: Top-Level Structure
docs.text:
{
  "id": "dashboard-id",
  "title": "Dashboard Title",
  "description": "Optional description",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "layout": { ... },
  "variables": [ ... ],
  "dataSources": [ ... ],
  "widgets": [ ... ],
  "theme": { ... }
}

docs.title: id (required)
docs.text:
Unique identifier for the dashboard. Used in URLs and API calls.

"id": "sales-q4-2024"

docs.title: title (required)
docs.text:
Display title shown in the dashboard header.

"title": "Q4 2024 Sales Dashboard"

docs.title: description (optional)
docs.text:
Longer description for documentation.

"description": "Tracks sales metrics and team performance for Q4 2024"

docs.title: version (optional)
docs.text:
Semantic version for tracking dashboard changes.

"version": "2.1.0"

docs.title: layout (required)
docs.text:
Defines how widgets are arranged.

"layout": {
  "type": "grid",
  "columns": 12,
  "rowHeight": 80,
  "gap": 16
}
Field Type Default Description type string "grid" Layout type (currently only "grid") columns number 12 Number of grid columns rowHeight number 80 Height of each grid row in pixels gap number 16 Gap between widgets in pixels

docs.title: variables (optional)
docs.text:
Dashboard-level variables for filtering and parameterization.

"variables": [
  {
    "id": "date_range",
    "type": "dateRange",
    "label": "Date Range",
    "default": { "start": "2024-01-01", "end": "2024-12-31" }
  },
  {
    "id": "region",
    "type": "select",
    "label": "Region",
    "options": ["North", "South", "East", "West"],
    "default": "North"
  }
]

See Variables for full reference.

docs.title: dataSources (required)
docs.text: Array of data source definitions. See Data Sources.

docs.title: widgets (required)
docs.text: Array of widget definitions. See Widgets.

docs.title: theme (optional)
docs.text:
Custom theme overrides.

"theme": {
  "colors": {
    "primary": "#4F46E5",
    "success": "#22C55E",
    "warning": "#F59E0B",
    "error": "#EF4444"
  },
  "fontFamily": "Inter, sans-serif"
}

docs.title: Variables
docs.text: Variables allow users to filter and parameterize dashboard data.

docs.title: text
docs.text:
Free-form text input.

{
  "id": "search",
  "type": "text",
  "label": "Search",
  "placeholder": "Enter search term...",
  "default": ""
}

docs.title: select
docs.text:
Single selection from options.

{
  "id": "status",
  "type": "select",
  "label": "Status",
  "options": ["active", "pending", "closed"],
  "default": "active"
}

docs.title: multiSelect
docs.text:
Multiple selection from options.

{
  "id": "categories",
  "type": "multiSelect",
  "label": "Categories",
  "options": ["Electronics", "Clothing", "Food"],
  "default": ["Electronics"]
}

docs.title: dateRange
docs.text:
Date range picker.

{
  "id": "period",
  "type": "dateRange",
  "label": "Time Period",
  "default": {
    "start": "2024-01-01",
    "end": "2024-12-31"
  }
}

docs.title: number
docs.text:
Numeric input with optional min/max.

{
  "id": "limit",
  "type": "number",
  "label": "Row Limit",
  "min": 1,
  "max": 1000,
  "default": 100
}

docs.title: Using Variables
docs.text:
Reference variables in queries and transforms using ${variableName} syntax:

{
  "id": "filtered-data",
  "type": "postgres",
  "query": "SELECT * FROM orders WHERE status = '${status}' AND created_at BETWEEN '${period.start}' AND '${period.end}'"
}

docs.title: Complete Example
docs.text:
{
  "id": "sales-dashboard",
  "title": "Sales Dashboard",
  "description": "Real-time sales metrics and trends",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "layout": {
    "type": "grid",
    "columns": 12,
    "rowHeight": 80
  },
  "variables": [
    {
      "id": "region",
      "type": "select",
      "label": "Region",
      "options": ["All", "North", "South", "East", "West"],
      "default": "All"
    }
  ],
  "dataSources": [
    {
      "id": "sales-summary",
      "type": "postgres",
      "query": "SELECT SUM(amount) as total, COUNT(*) as orders FROM sales WHERE region = '${region}' OR '${region}' = 'All'"
    }
  ],
  "widgets": [
    {
      "id": "total-sales",
      "type": "metric",
      "title": "Total Sales",
      "position": { "x": 0, "y": 0, "w": 4, "h": 2 },
      "dataSourceId": "sales-summary",
      "config": {
        "valueField": "total",
        "format": "currency"
      }
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Data Sources
docs.text: Data sources define where dashboard data comes from. Dashforge supports multiple data source types for different deployment scenarios.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Dashforge has two levels of data sources:

  1. Dashboard Data Sources - Defined in the dashboard JSON, these specify how widgets get their data (inline, URL, database query, etc.)
  2. External Data Sources - Server-side database connections (PostgreSQL, MySQL, etc.) managed via API that dashboard queries can reference

This page covers both. For the external data source API, see API Reference.

docs.title: Common Properties
docs.text:
All data sources share these properties:

{
  "id": "source-id",
  "type": "url",
  "refreshInterval": 60,
  "transform": [ ... ]
}
Property Type Required Description id string Yes Unique identifier for referencing type string Yes Data source type refreshInterval number No Auto-refresh interval in seconds transform array No Data transformations to apply

docs.title: Inline
docs.text:
Embed data directly in the dashboard JSON. Best for small, static datasets.

{
  "id": "categories",
  "type": "inline",
  "data": [
    { "name": "Electronics", "value": 450 },
    { "name": "Clothing", "value": 320 },
    { "name": "Food", "value": 280 },
    { "name": "Books", "value": 150 }
  ]
}

docs.title: URL
docs.text:
Fetch data from a URL (JSON or CSV). Works in static mode.

{
  "id": "sales-data",
  "type": "url",
  "url": "./data/sales.json",
  "format": "json",
  "refreshInterval": 300
}

docs.title: With Authentication
docs.text:
{
  "id": "api-data",
  "type": "url",
  "url": "https://api.example.com/data",
  "format": "json",
  "headers": {
    "Authorization": "Bearer ${API_TOKEN}"
  }
}

docs.title: CSV Format
docs.text:
{
  "id": "csv-data",
  "type": "url",
  "url": "./data/report.csv",
  "format": "csv",
  "csvOptions": {
    "delimiter": ",",
    "header": true
  }
}

docs.title: PostgreSQL
docs.text:
Query a PostgreSQL database. Requires server mode.

{
  "id": "orders",
  "type": "postgres",
  "query": "SELECT date, SUM(amount) as total, COUNT(*) as orders FROM orders WHERE status = 'completed' GROUP BY date ORDER BY date"
}

docs.title: With Parameters
docs.text:
Use dashboard variables in queries:

{
  "id": "filtered-orders",
  "type": "postgres",
  "query": "SELECT * FROM orders WHERE region = '${region}' AND created_at >= '${dateRange.start}' AND created_at <= '${dateRange.end}' ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT ${limit}"
}

SQL Injection

Variables are parameterized by the server to prevent SQL injection. Never concatenate user input directly into queries.

docs.title: Connection Reference
docs.text:
For multi-database setups:

{
  "id": "analytics",
  "type": "postgres",
  "connectionId": "analytics-db",
  "query": "SELECT * FROM events"
}

docs.title: Saved Query
docs.text:
Reference a saved query by ID (server mode only).

{
  "id": "monthly-report",
  "type": "savedQuery",
  "queryId": "q_abc123"
}

docs.title: Transforms
docs.text:
Apply transformations to data after fetching. See Transforms for full reference.

{
  "id": "top-products",
  "type": "postgres",
  "query": "SELECT * FROM products",
  "transform": [
    { "type": "sort", "config": { "field": "sales", "direction": "desc" } },
    { "type": "limit", "config": { "count": 10 } }
  ]
}

docs.title: Caching
docs.text:
Data sources support caching to reduce database load:

{
  "id": "expensive-query",
  "type": "postgres",
  "query": "SELECT ... complex aggregation ...",
  "cache": {
    "enabled": true,
    "ttl": 300,
    "key": "expensive-query-${region}"
  }
}

docs.title: Combining Data Sources
docs.text:
Widgets can reference multiple data sources:

{
  "dataSources": [
    {
      "id": "current-sales",
      "type": "postgres",
      "query": "SELECT SUM(amount) as current FROM sales WHERE year = 2024"
    },
    {
      "id": "previous-sales",
      "type": "postgres",
      "query": "SELECT SUM(amount) as previous FROM sales WHERE year = 2023"
    }
  ],
  "widgets": [
    {
      "id": "yoy-comparison",
      "type": "metric",
      "dataSourceId": "current-sales",
      "config": {
        "valueField": "current",
        "comparison": {
          "dataSourceId": "previous-sales",
          "field": "previous",
          "type": "percentage"
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
Configure error behavior:

{
  "id": "data",
  "type": "url",
  "url": "https://api.example.com/data",
  "onError": {
    "behavior": "fallback",
    "fallbackData": [],
    "message": "Unable to load data"
  }
}
Behavior Description fail Show error message (default) fallback Use fallback data retry Retry with backoff ignore Show empty state

docs.title: External Data Sources (Server Mode)
docs.text: In server mode, Dashforge supports connecting to external databases through a plugin-style provider system. This allows dashboards to query multiple databases like Metabase.

docs.title: Supported Providers
docs.text: Provider Status Description postgres Stable PostgreSQL 12+ mysql Stable MySQL 5.7+ / MariaDB 10.3+ clickhouse Planned ClickHouse analytics database duckdb Planned DuckDB embedded analytics

docs.title: Creating a Data Source
docs.text:
Use the API to register external database connections:

curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/v1/datasources \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "name": "Production Analytics",
    "slug": "prod-analytics",
    "type": "postgres",
    "connectionUrl": "postgres://user:pass@db.example.com:5432/analytics?sslmode=require",
    "maxConnections": 10,
    "queryTimeoutSeconds": 30,
    "readOnly": true,
    "sslEnabled": true,
    "active": true
  }'

docs.title: Using Environment Variables
docs.text:
For security, connection URLs can reference environment variables:

{
  "name": "Production DB",
  "slug": "prod-db",
  "type": "postgres",
  "connectionUrlEnv": "PROD_DATABASE_URL",
  "readOnly": true
}

The server reads the URL from PROD_DATABASE_URL at connection time.

docs.title: Testing Connections
docs.text:
Before using a data source, test the connection:

curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/v1/datasources/1/test

Response:

{
  "success": true,
  "durationMs": 45
}

docs.title: Executing Queries
docs.text:
Query an external data source:

curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/v1/datasources/1/query \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "query": "SELECT date, SUM(amount) as total FROM sales GROUP BY date ORDER BY date",
    "parameters": {},
    "maxRows": 1000
  }'

Response:

{
  "columns": [
    {"name": "date", "type": "DATE", "nullable": false},
    {"name": "total", "type": "NUMERIC", "nullable": true}
  ],
  "rows": [
    {"date": "2024-01-01", "total": 15000},
    {"date": "2024-01-02", "total": 18500}
  ],
  "rowCount": 2,
  "executionTimeMs": 125
}

docs.title: Schema Introspection
docs.text:
Get the database schema for a data source:

curl "http://localhost:8080/api/v1/datasources/1/schema?columns=true"

Response:

{
  "tables": [
    {
      "schema": "public",
      "name": "sales",
      "type": "table",
      "columns": [
        {"name": "id", "type": "INTEGER", "nullable": false},
        {"name": "date", "type": "DATE", "nullable": false},
        {"name": "amount", "type": "NUMERIC", "nullable": true}
      ]
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Read-Only Mode
docs.text:
When readOnly: true, the data source only allows SELECT queries:

{
  "readOnly": true
}

Attempting INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE returns an error:

{
  "error": "write operation not allowed on read-only connection"
}

Best Practice

Always set readOnly: true for dashboard data sources. Use a database user with read-only permissions as an additional safeguard.

docs.title: Connection Pooling
docs.text:
Each data source maintains its own connection pool:

Setting Default Description maxConnections 10 Maximum open connections queryTimeoutSeconds 30 Query execution timeout

Connection pools are managed automatically. Connections are reused across queries and closed when the data source is deleted or the server shuts down.

docs.title: Provider Capabilities
docs.text:
Query available providers and their capabilities:

curl http://localhost:8080/api/v1/datasources/providers

Response:

{
  "providers": [
    {
      "name": "postgres",
      "capabilities": {
        "supportsTransactions": true,
        "supportsStreaming": true,
        "supportsPreparedStmts": true,
        "supportsNamedParams": false,
        "parameterStyle": "positional_dollar"
      }
    },
    {
      "name": "mysql",
      "capabilities": {
        "supportsTransactions": true,
        "supportsStreaming": true,
        "supportsPreparedStmts": true,
        "supportsNamedParams": false,
        "parameterStyle": "positional_question"
      }
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Parameter Styles
docs.text:
Different databases use different parameter placeholders:

Provider Style Example postgres Positional dollar $1, $2, $3 mysql Positional question ?, ?, ?

When using named parameters (:name or @name), Dashforge automatically converts them to the provider's native style.

docs.title: Custom Providers
docs.text:
Developers can register custom providers programmatically:

import "github.com/plexusone/dashforge/datasource"

// Implement the Provider interface
type MyProvider struct{}

func (p *MyProvider) Name() string { return "mydb" }
func (p *MyProvider) Connect(ctx context.Context, cfg datasource.ConnectionConfig) (datasource.Connection, error) { ... }
func (p *MyProvider) ValidateConfig(cfg datasource.ConnectionConfig) error { ... }
func (p *MyProvider) Capabilities() datasource.Capabilities { ... }

// Register at startup
datasource.Register(&MyProvider{})

// Or inject into manager for runtime registration
manager.RegisterCustomProvider(&MyProvider{})

See the Development guide for more details on extending Dashforge.

docs.title: Development
docs.text: This guide covers setting up a development environment and contributing to Dashforge.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Go 1.22 or later
  • Node.js 18+ (for builder and viewer development)
  • PostgreSQL 14+ (for server mode)
  • Docker (optional, for containerized development)

docs.title: Clone the Repository
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/plexusone/dashforge.git
cd dashforge

docs.title: Build
docs.text:
# Build all Go binaries
go build ./...

# Build specific binary
go build -o dashforge ./cmd/dashforge
go build -o dashforge-server ./cmd/dashforge-server

# Build the dashboard builder
cd builder && npm install && npm run build && cd ..

docs.title: Run Tests
docs.text:
# Go tests
go test -v ./...

# With coverage
go test -coverprofile=coverage.out ./...
go tool cover -html=coverage.out

# Builder tests
cd builder && npm run typecheck && cd ..

docs.title: Lint
docs.text:
# Go
golangci-lint run

# Builder
cd builder && npm run lint && cd ..

docs.title: Project Structure
docs.text:
dashforge/
├── cmd/
│   ├── dashforge/           # Static CLI
│   └── dashforge-server/    # Full server
├── builder/                 # Dashboard builder (React)
│   ├── src/
│   │   ├── components/      # React components
│   │   │   ├── canvas/      # Grid layout canvas
│   │   │   ├── palette/     # Widget palette
│   │   │   ├── properties/  # Properties panel
│   │   │   ├── chart-builder/   # Chart configuration
│   │   │   ├── query-builder/   # Cube.js queries
│   │   │   ├── schema-browser/  # Schema explorer
│   │   │   └── widgets/     # Widget renderers
│   │   ├── stores/          # Zustand state
│   │   ├── api/             # API clients
│   │   ├── ai/              # AI generation
│   │   └── types/           # TypeScript types
│   ├── package.json
│   └── vite.config.ts
├── cube/                    # Cube.js semantic layer
│   ├── model/cubes/         # Data models (YAML)
│   └── cube.js              # Cube configuration
├── dashboardir/             # Dashboard JSON types
├── datasource/              # Data source providers
│   ├── providers/           # PostgreSQL, MySQL
│   ├── manager.go           # Connection pool
│   └── query.go             # Query executor
├── ent/                     # Ent ORM
│   └── schema/              # Entity schemas
├── internal/server/         # Server implementation
│   ├── api/                 # REST API handlers
│   ├── auth/                # JWT & OAuth
│   └── db/                  # Database layer
├── viewer/                  # Static HTML/JS viewer
├── docs/                    # MkDocs documentation
└── examples/                # Example dashboards

docs.title: Builder Development
docs.text: The dashboard builder is a React/TypeScript application.

docs.title: Development Server
docs.text:
cd builder
npm install
npm run dev
# Opens http://localhost:5173 with hot reload

The dev server proxies /api requests to http://localhost:8080, so start the Dashforge server:

# In another terminal
go run ./cmd/dashforge-server serve --port 8080

docs.title: Building for Production
docs.text:
cd builder
npm run build
# Output in builder/dist/

The built files are embedded in the Go binary via builder/embed.go.

docs.title: Type Checking
docs.text:
cd builder
npm run typecheck

docs.title: Key Technologies
docs.text: Technology Purpose React 18 UI framework TypeScript Type safety Vite Build tool Tailwind CSS Styling Zustand State management React Query Data fetching react-grid-layout Drag-and-drop grid ECharts Chart rendering Cube.js Client Semantic queries

docs.title: Adding a New Widget Type
docs.text:
1. Add type to builder/src/types/dashboard.ts
  2. Create widget component in builder/src/components/widgets/
  3. Add to WidgetContainer.tsx switch statement
  4. Add palette entry in WidgetPalette.tsx
  5. Add config editor in PropertiesPanel.tsx

docs.title: Adding a New Chart Type
docs.text:
1. Add geometry to ChartConfig in types/dashboard.ts
  2. Add option to GeometryPicker.tsx
  3. Add rendering case in ChartWidget.tsx
  4. Update DataMapping.tsx for encoding requirements

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
cd cube
npm install

# Create .env
cat > .env << EOF
CUBEJS_DB_TYPE=postgres
CUBEJS_DB_HOST=localhost
CUBEJS_DB_NAME=your_database
CUBEJS_DB_USER=your_user
CUBEJS_DB_PASS=your_password
EOF

docs.title: Running
docs.text:
cd cube
npm run dev
# API at http://localhost:4000

docs.title: Adding Data Models
docs.text:
Create YAML files in cube/model/cubes/:

cubes:
  - name: MyTable
    sql: SELECT * FROM my_table

    measures:
      - name: count
        type: count

    dimensions:
      - name: id
        type: string
        primaryKey: true

docs.title: Making Changes
docs.text:
1. Create a feature branch
  2. Make changes
  3. Run tests and linter
  4. Submit pull request

docs.title: Ent Schema Changes
docs.text:
When modifying Ent schemas:

# Edit schema files in ent/schema/

# Regenerate code
go generate ./ent

# Run migrations (dev database)
./dashforge-server serve --database-url "$DEV_DATABASE_URL" --auto-migrate

docs.title: Adding a New API Endpoint
docs.text:
1. Add handler in internal/server/api/
  2. Register route in handler setup
  3. Add documentation in docs/api-reference.md
  4. Add tests

docs.title: Adding a New Data Source Provider
docs.text:
1. Create provider package in datasource/providers/<name>/
  2. Implement the Provider interface
  3. Register via init()
  4. Import in internal/server/server.go

Example: ClickHouse Provider

// datasource/providers/clickhouse/provider.go
package clickhouse

import (
    "context"
    "database/sql"

    _ "github.com/ClickHouse/clickhouse-go/v2"
    "github.com/plexusone/dashforge/datasource"
)

func init() {
    datasource.Register(&Provider{})
}

type Provider struct{}

func (p *Provider) Name() string { return "clickhouse" }

func (p *Provider) Connect(ctx context.Context, cfg datasource.ConnectionConfig) (datasource.Connection, error) {
    db, err := sql.Open("clickhouse", cfg.ConnectionURL)
    if err != nil {
        return nil, err
    }
    return &Connection{db: db}, nil
}

docs.title: Database
docs.text:
# Start PostgreSQL with Docker
docker run -d \
  --name dashforge-db \
  -e POSTGRES_USER=dashforge \
  -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=password \
  -e POSTGRES_DB=dashforge \
  -p 5432:5432 \
  postgres:16-alpine

# Connection string
export DATABASE_URL="postgres://dashforge:password@localhost:5432/dashforge?sslmode=disable"

docs.title: Full Stack
docs.text:
# Terminal 1: Dashforge server
export JWT_SECRET="dev-secret-key-at-least-32-chars"
export DATABASE_URL="postgres://dashforge:password@localhost:5432/dashforge?sslmode=disable"
go run ./cmd/dashforge-server serve --auto-migrate

# Terminal 2: Builder dev server (optional, for hot reload)
cd builder && npm run dev

# Terminal 3: Cube.js (optional, for semantic queries)
cd cube && npm run dev

Access:

  • Builder (dev): http://localhost:5173
  • Builder (embedded): http://localhost:8080/builder/
  • Viewer: http://localhost:8080/viewer/
  • Cube.js Playground: http://localhost:4000

docs.title: Unit Tests
docs.text: go test -v ./internal/...

docs.title: Integration Tests
docs.text:
export TEST_DATABASE_URL="postgres://..."
go test -v -tags=integration ./...

docs.title: Builder Type Check
docs.text: cd builder && npm run typecheck

docs.title: Go
docs.text:
• Follow standard Go conventions
  • Use gofmt for formatting
  • Use golangci-lint for linting
  • Keep functions focused and small
  • Prefer returning errors over logging

docs.title: TypeScript
docs.text:
• Use TypeScript strict mode
  • Prefer functional components
  • Use React Query for data fetching
  • Use Zustand for state management

docs.title: Commits
docs.text:
Follow Conventional Commits:

feat(builder): add scatter plot support
fix(api): handle null values in query results
docs: update builder documentation
chore(deps): update dependencies

docs.title: Building Docs
docs.text:
# Install MkDocs
pip install mkdocs-material

# Serve locally
mkdocs serve

# Build static site
mkdocs build

docs.title: Deploying Docs
docs.text: mkdocs gh-deploy

docs.title: Release Process
docs.text:
1. Update version in relevant files
  2. Update CHANGELOG.md
  3. Build and test: 
    go build ./...
go test ./...
cd builder && npm run build && npm run typecheck && cd ..
  4. Create release commit and tag
  5. Push to trigger release workflow
git add -A
git commit -m "chore: release v1.2.0"
git tag v1.2.0
git push origin main --tags

docs.title: Server Logging
docs.text:
export LOG_LEVEL=debug
./dashforge-server serve

docs.title: Builder DevTools
docs.text: React DevTools and Redux DevTools (for Zustand) are available in development mode.

docs.title: Cube.js Playground
docs.text: Access http://localhost:4000 for the Cube.js development playground with query builder and schema explorer.

docs.title: Builder Build Fails
docs.text:
# Clear node_modules and reinstall
cd builder
rm -rf node_modules package-lock.json
npm install
npm run build

docs.title: Embed Error (no matching files)
docs.text:
The dist/ directory must exist for Go embed to work:

cd builder && npm run build && cd ..

docs.title: Ent Generation Fails

rm -rf ent/*.go ent/*/
go generate ./ent

psql "$DATABASE_URL" -c "SELECT 1"
docker logs dashforge-db

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: This guide walks you through installing Dashforge and creating your first dashboard.

docs.title: From Source
docs.text:
# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/plexusone/dashforge.git
cd dashforge

# Build the binaries
go build -o dashforge ./cmd/dashforge
go build -o dashforge-server ./cmd/dashforge-server

# Build the dashboard builder (optional)
cd builder && npm install && npm run build && cd ..

docs.title: Go Install
docs.text:
go install github.com/plexusone/dashforge/cmd/dashforge@latest
go install github.com/plexusone/dashforge/cmd/dashforge-server@latest

docs.title: Visual Builder Quick Start
docs.text: The fastest way to create dashboards is with the visual builder.

docs.title: 1. Build the Builder
docs.text:
cd builder
npm install
npm run build
cd ..

docs.title: 2. Start the Server
docs.text: go run ./cmd/dashforge-server serve --port 8080

docs.title: 3. Open the Builder
docs.text: Navigate to http://localhost:8080/builder/ in your browser.

docs.title: 4. Create Your Dashboard
docs.text:
1. Drag widgets from the left palette onto the canvas
  2. Click a widget to edit its properties in the right panel
  3. Configure chart types, data mappings, and styles
  4. Click "Save" to save your dashboard

See Dashboard Builder for detailed documentation.

docs.title: Static Mode Quick Start
docs.text: For simple dashboards without a server.

docs.title: 1. Create a Dashboard JSON File
docs.text:
Create my-dashboard.json:

{
  "id": "my-first-dashboard",
  "title": "My First Dashboard",
  "layout": {
    "type": "grid",
    "columns": 12,
    "rowHeight": 80
  },
  "dataSources": [
    {
      "id": "sample-data",
      "type": "inline",
      "data": [
        { "month": "Jan", "sales": 100, "profit": 20 },
        { "month": "Feb", "sales": 150, "profit": 35 },
        { "month": "Mar", "sales": 200, "profit": 50 },
        { "month": "Apr", "sales": 180, "profit": 40 }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "widgets": [
    {
      "id": "sales-metric",
      "type": "metric",
      "title": "Total Sales",
      "position": { "x": 0, "y": 0, "w": 3, "h": 2 },
      "datasourceId": "sample-data",
      "config": {
        "valueField": "sales",
        "format": "currency"
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "sales-chart",
      "type": "chart",
      "title": "Monthly Sales",
      "position": { "x": 3, "y": 0, "w": 9, "h": 4 },
      "datasourceId": "sample-data",
      "config": {
        "geometry": "bar",
        "encodings": { "x": "month", "y": "sales" },
        "style": { "showLegend": true }
      }
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: 2. Serve Locally
docs.text:
# Using Python's built-in server
cd dashforge
python3 -m http.server 8080

# Open in browser
open http://localhost:8080/viewer/?dashboard=../my-dashboard.json

docs.title: Server Mode Quick Start
docs.text: For dynamic data sources, authentication, and multi-tenancy.

docs.title: 1. Set Up PostgreSQL
docs.text:
# Create database
createdb dashforge

# Set environment variables
export DATABASE_URL="postgres://localhost:5432/dashforge?sslmode=disable"
export JWT_SECRET="your-secure-secret-key-at-least-32-chars"

docs.title: 2. Start the Server
docs.text:
./dashforge-server serve \
  --port 8080 \
  --database-url "$DATABASE_URL" \
  --jwt-secret "$JWT_SECRET" \
  --auto-migrate

docs.title: 3. Access Dashforge
docs.text:
• Builder: http://localhost:8080/builder/ - Visual dashboard editor
  • Viewer: http://localhost:8080/viewer/ - Dashboard viewer
  • API: http://localhost:8080/api/v1/ - REST API

docs.title: Adding Cube.js (Optional)
docs.text: For semantic queries with business-friendly field names:

docs.title: 1. Configure Cube.js

cd cube
npm install

# Create .env file
cat > .env << EOF
CUBEJS_DB_TYPE=postgres
CUBEJS_DB_HOST=localhost
CUBEJS_DB_PORT=5432
CUBEJS_DB_NAME=your_database
CUBEJS_DB_USER=your_user
CUBEJS_DB_PASS=your_password
CUBEJS_API_SECRET=your-api-secret
EOF

docs.title: 2. Start Cube.js

npm run dev
# Cube.js API available at http://localhost:4000

docs.title: 3. Use in Builder

The builder automatically connects to Cube.js for schema introspection and query building.

See Cube.js Integration for details.

docs.title: Adding OAuth (Optional)
docs.text:
To enable GitHub and Google login:

export GITHUB_CLIENT_ID="your-github-client-id"
export GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET="your-github-client-secret"
export GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID="your-google-client-id"
export GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET="your-google-client-secret"
export BASE_URL="http://localhost:8080"

./dashforge-server serve \
  --port 8080 \
  --database-url "$DATABASE_URL" \
  --jwt-secret "$JWT_SECRET" \
  --auto-migrate
